content.Title: AHA/ACC/HHS Strategies to Enhance Application of Clinical Practice Guidelines in Patients With Cardiovascular Disease and Comorbid Conditions
content.Source: American Heart Association, American College of Cardiology, and U.S. Department of Health and Human Services
content.Background: Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of death in the United States and worldwide, resulting in substantial suffering and healthcare-related expenditures. For over 30 years, the American Heart Association (AHA) and the American College of Cardiology (ACC) have collaborated to provide the best available scientific evidence through clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) for cardiovascular conditions.
content.Purpose: The guidelines aim to optimize care for patients with cardiovascular conditions by reflecting on the evolution of relevant foundational domains such as biomedical discoveries and public policy. They address the increasing prevalence of multiple chronic conditions among American adults and Medicare beneficiaries.
content.Prevalence Data: More than a quarter of American adults have ≥2 chronic conditions, while among Medicare beneficiaries, over two-thirds (68%) have ≥2 chronic conditions, and 14% have ≥6 chronic conditions.

content.Title: Comorbidities and CPGs for Cardiovascular Conditions
content.Source: The Journal of the American College of Cardiology (JACC), Vol 64, No 17, 2014
content.Overview: The clinical guideline discusses the integration of comorbidities into Clinical Practice Guidelines (CPGs) developed by AHA/ACC for cardiovascular disease management.
Key Points: CPGs are focused on the prevention, diagnosis, and management of ischemic heart disease, heart failure, and atrial fibrillation.
Certain CPGs provide special considerations related to myocardial infarction, pregnancy, hyperthyroidism, etc.
There is a growing need to address comorbidities in cardiovascular patients, particularly in older adults.
AHA/ACC has started to address comorbidities following recommendations from the US Department of Health and Human Services.
The guideline discusses the prevalence of common comorbidities in Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 and older with cardiovascular conditions.
content.Data Source: Based on Medicare beneficiaries' claims data.

content.The document discusses common comorbidit: The document discusses common comorbidities associated with four major cardiovascular conditions in Medicare beneficiaries aged 65 years or older. The conditions discussed are ischemic heart disease, heart failure, atrial fibrillation, and stroke. It highlights the most prevalent comorbidities, including hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes mellitus, arthritis, anemia, chronic kidney disease, and Alzheimer's disease/dementia. It also emphasizes the complexity of managing patients with multiple chronic conditions. The future directions include addressing the challenges in developing and implementing clinical practice guidelines (CPGs) for patients with multiple comorbidities, as they often face barriers in clinical trials.

content.summary: The document discusses the importance of including patients with multiple comorbid conditions in clinical trials and clinical practice guidelines (CPGs). It highlights the role of the U.S. Food and Drug Administration in ensuring these patients are not unnecessarily excluded. It addresses challenges in incorporating comorbidities into CPGs and the need for individualized, patient-centered decision-making. The collaboration between the AHA/ACC and the Department of Health and Human Services aims to improve recommendations for managing common cardiovascular comorbidities. The document also recognizes the increasing use of electronic health records for continuous evaluation and the need to address common comorbidities like obesity, depression, and cognitive impairment.

content.guideline_information: Relationships in this table are arranged alphabetically within each category and are modeled unless otherwise noted. A person is deemed to have a significant interest in a business if the interest represents ownership >= 5% of the voting stock or share of the business entity or funds received from the business entity exceed 5% of the person's gross income for the previous year.
